metadata.description: Botanical identifications for Voynich Manuscript herbal folios
sources: Tucker & Janick (2018) - Flora of the Voynich Codex (Springer)
Ethel Voynich / O'Neill (1944) / Petersen - Earliest identifications
metadata.eva_transcription: Zandbergen-Landini (ZL), cross-referenced with Takahashi (;H lines in LSI)

confidence_scoring.high: 0.85+ — Multiple independent researchers agree, distinctive morphology
confidence_scoring.medium-high: 0.75-0.84 — Two+ researchers agree with strong evidence
confidence_scoring.medium: 0.60-0.74 — Two researchers agree or one strong morphological match
confidence_scoring.low: <0.40 — Single proposer, controversial, or weak evidence (excluded)

anchors.medieval_source_texts: Macer Floridus (Viola, in Spuria); Circa instans (Viola); Dioscorides Latin (Viola)
anchors.notes: One of most widely accepted IDs; multiple independent proposers; Wikipedia notes "reasonable certainty"

anchors.medieval_source_texts: Circa instans (Nigella); Dioscorides Latin (Melanthion > Melanthium); various medieval herbals (Git/Gith)
anchors.notes: Bax decoded text as "kaur" = black; morphology matches; two independent proposers

anchors.medieval_source_texts: Circa instans (Nenuphar); Pseudo-Apuleius ch.68 (Nimfa); Dioscorides Latin (Nymphaea)
anchors.notes: One of earliest accepted IDs; multiple proposers agree on aquatic plant
Multiple proposers; distinctive carnivorous morphology widely noted
Two proposers agree; common medicinal herb

anchors.medieval_source_texts: Pseudo-Apuleius ch.115 (Cannabis silvatica); Dioscorides Latin (Cannabis); Circa instans (Cannabis)
anchors.notes: Two proposers agree; palmate leaves distinctive
Multiple proposers agree on Solanaceae family
Two proposers agree; but tulips arrived in Europe late 15th/16th c.
Two proposers agree; important dye plant
Two proposers agree; distinctive round seed pods
Two proposers across time; high-value spice; recognizable morphology

anchors.medieval_latin_name: Elleborus; Elleborus niger; Elleborus albus; Veratrum
anchors.medieval_source_texts: Dioscorides Latin (Elleborus niger, Elleborus albus); Circa instans (Elleborus)
anchors.notes: Bax identified via text decoding + morphology; multiple proposers
Two proposers agree; common culinary herb
Two proposers agree; distinctive spurred flowers

anchors.medieval_source_texts: Dioscorides Latin (Mandragora, with male/female distinction); Circa instans (Mandragora); Pseudo-Apuleius (not in standard text; added in some MSS)
anchors.notes: Two proposers agree; anthropomorphic root drawing common in herbals
Two proposers agree; common medicinal herb
Two proposers agree; important medicinal herb

anchors.medieval_source_texts: Pseudo-Apuleius ch.102 (Salvia); Macer Floridus ch.24 (Salvia); Circa instans (Salvia); Dioscorides (Elelisphakon > Salvia in Latin)
anchors.notes: Two proposers agree; major medicinal herb
Early ID confirmed by later researcher

anchors.medieval_source_texts: Dioscorides Latin (Ricinus, Cici, Croton); Pseudo-Apuleius (not in standard text)

anchors.notes: Two proposers agree; well-known medicinal herb
One of earliest and most famous IDs; confirmed by Guy (1991); disputed by Stolfi; New World plant
Two proposers agree; distinctive frond morphology
Genus-level agreement is common; distinctive pipe-shaped flowers

anchors.medieval_source_texts: Pseudo-Apuleius ch.53 (Papaver silvaticum); Dioscorides Latin (Papaver, Mecon); Circa instans (Opium, Papaver)
Circa instans (Nenuphar); Pseudo-Apuleius ch.68 (Nimfa); Dioscorides Latin (Nymphaea)

anchors.notes: One of earliest IDs; upper right corner; New World; "no longer generally accepted" per voynich.nu
Note: same ID as f26r; possibly a variant drawing
Fern; possibly the "maidenhair fern" cited by Wikipedia as certain
